lessons.metaphor_seed: A personal trainer isn't paid for the number of push-ups they make you do (output) — they're paid because you actually get fitter, lose the weight, or run the marathon (outcome). 200 push-ups that leave you no healthier is pure output and zero outcome.
lessons.case_study_seed: Amazon's 'Working Backwards' PR/FAQ process: teams write the customer-facing press release and FAQ before building anything, forcing them to define the outcome first and only build if it's compelling. Contrast with the 'feature factory' anti-pattern of celebrating launches regardless of whether any metric moved.
lessons.pm_angle: Technical-literacy angle: outcomes are measured through instrumentation — the analytics and event-tracking that engineers build into the product. To own outcomes you need to understand how usage data is captured, what's technically feasible to measure, and why 'we shipped it' is only a bet until the metric moves.
deep_sections: Why 'outcomes are hard' — and the cargo-cult trap of renaming a roadmap
How a shipped feature is a bet, not a result
The bridge from outcomes to OKRs

lessons.title: The Product Trio: PM, Designer, and Engineers as One Unit
lessons.summary: Great products come from three perspectives working together continuously, not from an assembly line of handoffs. The PM owns value and viability, the designer owns usability, and engineers own feasibility — and crucially, engineers belong in the room inventing solutions, not just receiving specs.
lessons.metaphor_seed: Making a film: the director (PM) owns the vision and whether the story moves audiences and makes money; the cinematographer (designer) owns whether it looks right and is easy to follow; the effects and technical crew (engineers) own whether the ambitious shots are buildable. They must argue and invent together from day one, not receive a finished shot-list 'over the wall.'
lessons.case_study_seed: Apple's iPhone: engineering's multi-touch feasibility breakthrough, Jony Ive's usability and industrial design, and Jobs's product/business vision were inseparable — hardware, software, and experience were co-discovered, not handed off. SVPG's archetype of tight trio collaboration.
lessons.pm_angle: Technical-literacy angle: Cagan's line 'if you're just using your engineers to code, you're only getting about half their value.' To pull that other half out you must be able to discuss feasibility, trade-offs, and architecture well enough that engineers want you in their discovery conversations.
deep_sections: Who owns which of the four risks
Why 'over the wall' handoffs produce expensive flops
How Teresa Torres popularized the 'trio' term Cagan described in INSPIRED

lessons.title: Real PM vs. Product Management Theater
lessons.summary: Most people with 'product owner' or even 'product manager' titles are administering a backlog and writing tickets — going through the motions Cagan calls 'product management theater.' A real PM has deep knowledge of the customer, the data, and the business, and owns the hardest risks: value and viability.
lessons.metaphor_seed: A head chef versus a waiter: the real PM is the chef who tastes the food, knows the diners' palates, watches costs and suppliers, and decides what's worth cooking. The backlog administrator is a waiter relaying shouted orders to the kitchen — necessary motion, no judgment about whether the menu is any good. Theater is a waiter in a chef's hat who never touches the stove.
lessons.case_study_seed: Almosafer (Saudi travel company): built genuine product-manager muscle from 2018 — continuous customer interviews, data analysis, rapid prototyping for regional needs — reaching 70%+ regional market share and a billion-dollar airline business, outcompeting global giants. Cagan's 'Product Management Theater' essay is the canonical critique of the fake version.
lessons.pm_angle: Technical-literacy angle: 'deep knowledge of the data' means being able to query, read dashboards, and reason about what the numbers actually say — a quantitative, semi-technical skill. As AI automates ticket-writing busywork, this analytical judgment is exactly what separates a real PM from a theater PM.
deep_sections: The three deep competencies: customer, data, business/industry
Why 'Product Owner' (a Scrum role) is not a Product Manager
Why the PM is not the boss of the trio

modules.title: Module 2 — Product Discovery: Figuring Out What's Worth Building
modules.goal: Master the core craft of de-risking ideas cheaply and fast before spending expensive engineering time. Learn the four big risks, build the habit of continuous customer contact, and use the visual tools that connect everything you build back to an outcome.

lessons.title: Discovery vs. Delivery: Two Tracks, One Team
lessons.summary: Deciding whether to build something at all (discovery) is a completely different activity from building it well (delivery), and the best teams run both continuously and in parallel — with the same people. Most product failures come not from building badly but from building the wrong thing well.
lessons.metaphor_seed: Opening a restaurant: delivery is the kitchen running smoothly every night (reliable, repeatable, high quality); discovery is the chef quietly testing tomorrow's specials on a few regulars. The smart restaurant does both at once with the same crew — one that only runs the kitchen serves a stale menu, one that only experiments never feeds anyone.
lessons.case_study_seed: Spotify's published 'Thoughtful Execution Framework,' which runs discovery (qualitative tests and instrumentation analysis of the sign-up flow) in parallel with delivery rather than guessing at features and shipping blindly.
lessons.pm_angle: Technical-literacy angle: 'dual-track' does NOT mean a discovery team handing a spec to a delivery team — the lead engineer is in discovery so feasibility is considered from day one. You need enough technical fluency to keep that engineer engaged in the 'should we?' conversation, not just the 'how?'.
deep_sections: What dual-track agile is — and the handoff trap Cagan warns about
How validated discovery feeds delivery, and usage data feeds back into discovery
Avoiding both 'ship junk fast' and 'analysis paralysis'

lessons.title: The Four Big Risks: How Every Idea Can Fail
lessons.summary: Cagan's four big risks are the canonical checklist for what discovery must address before building: will customers buy/use it (value), can they figure it out (usability), can we build it (feasibility), and does it work for the business (viability). Naming them gives a team a shared language for 'what could make this fail, and have we tested it?'
lessons.metaphor_seed: Before greenlighting a $200M blockbuster, a studio asks: will anyone buy a ticket (value), will the audience follow the plot (usability), can we film these stunts on budget (feasibility), and does the whole thing make money and fit our brand after marketing and theater cuts (viability). Skipping any one is how studios produce expensive flops.
lessons.case_study_seed: CB Insights' widely-cited finding that 42% of startups fail from 'no market need' — value risk made concrete. Contrast with the SVPG-style coaching example of an HR-tech team that built AI candidate-ranking for six months before discovering HR managers distrusted the black-box output (a value-and-viability failure).
lessons.pm_angle: Technical-literacy angle: feasibility risk is tested through engineering spikes and technical prototypes, and the most common imbalance is teams over-indexing on feasibility (engineers love technical spikes) while neglecting value and viability. You need to recognize that pattern and rebalance the team's attention.
deep_sections: Which teammate owns which risk
Why teams over-test the easy risks (usability) and under-test the dangerous ones (value, viability)
Matching the test type to the risk

lessons.title: Continuous Discovery: A Weekly Habit, Not a Phase
lessons.summary: Discovery is a weekly rhythm of customer contact, not a one-time project kickoff. Teresa Torres's definition: weekly touchpoints with customers, by the team building the product, running small research activities in pursuit of a desired outcome. This habit separates evidence-driven leaders from opinion-driven ones.
lessons.metaphor_seed: The difference between cramming for one annual medical check-up versus wearing a fitness tracker every day. The annual physical is out of date the moment you leave the clinic; the daily tracker catches small changes early, builds a trend line, and lets you adjust this week — not next year.
lessons.case_study_seed: Stack Overflow's platform team documented (July 2025) adopting continuous discovery — a steady weekly cadence of customer conversations to shape their platform roadmap — as a deliberate change from sporadic, ad-hoc research.
lessons.pm_angle: Technical-literacy angle: the weekly loop pairs qualitative interviews with quantitative signal from product instrumentation. Knowing what your analytics can and can't tell you — and how to triangulate a customer story against the usage data — is a semi-technical skill that makes your interviews far sharper.
deep_sections: Story-based interviewing: 'tell me about the last time you...' instead of opinions
The interview snapshot so learning accumulates instead of being lost
Research questions vs. the single interview question you actually ask

lessons.title: The Opportunity Solution Tree: Mapping Outcomes to Ideas
lessons.summary: The opportunity solution tree is Torres's signature visual: a single measurable outcome at the root, customer problems (opportunities) branching beneath it, multiple candidate solutions under each, and small assumption tests under those. It forces you to explore many problems and many solutions before committing — directly countering the PM's habit of falling for the first idea.
lessons.metaphor_seed: A family tree, but for ideas: at the top sits your one true goal ('more people finish signing up'); branching beneath are the frustrations blocking it ('I don't trust giving my card,' 'the form is too long'); beneath each hang several possible fixes. You don't marry the first idea you meet — you survey the whole family and pick the most promising lineage.
lessons.case_study_seed: Spotify uses OST-style discovery to drive 'improve user retention and engagement,' branching into opportunities like 'personalize the listening experience' and comparing multiple solutions (better discovery, personalized playlists) rather than shipping one feature on a hunch.
lessons.pm_angle: Technical-literacy angle: the root of every tree is a metric your team can actually influence and instrument. Choosing a measurable, attributable outcome (vs. a vanity number) requires understanding how the product is instrumented and what counts as a clean signal — and in 2026, AI can help draft and maintain trees, but you must own which opportunities matter.
deep_sections: The four layers: outcome, opportunities, solutions, assumption tests
Picking which branch to pursue (importance, size, fit to the outcome)
Keeping the tree a living map, not a one-time deliverable

lessons.title: Assumption Mapping: Find the One Belief That Could Sink You
lessons.summary: Every idea rests on hidden beliefs that must be true for it to work. Assumption mapping drags those beliefs into the open and ranks them by importance versus evidence, so you test the one 'leap of faith' that would sink everything — rather than wasting time validating safe, obvious things.
lessons.metaphor_seed: Before crossing a frozen lake you don't randomly tap the ice everywhere — you find the one spot in the middle where, if the ice is thin, you definitely fall through, and you tap that first. The 2x2 grid asks which beliefs are both load-bearing (you'll fall if wrong) and untested (you haven't tapped them yet).
lessons.case_study_seed: Airbnb's founders' riskiest assumption was 'strangers will pay to sleep in another stranger's home.' Rather than build a full platform, they tested exactly that belief with a hacked-together site and air mattresses at a 2007 San Francisco design conference, and got three paying guests — validating the one leap of faith everything else depended on.
lessons.pm_angle: Technical-literacy angle: assumptions span five categories including feasibility ('can we actually build this with our data and tech?') and ethical risk. You need enough technical judgment to spot which feasibility assumptions are genuine leaps of faith versus routine, and to know when an engineering spike is the right test.
deep_sections: The five assumption categories: desirability, viability, feasibility, usability, ethical
The 2x2 map: importance vs. evidence, and the top-right 'leap of faith' zone
Riskiest Assumption Test (RAT) vs. MVP

lessons.title: Cheap Tests: Prototypes, Fake Doors, and Wizard of Oz
lessons.summary: Once you know your riskiest assumption, the art is finding the cheapest, fastest way to test it without building the real thing. A menu of techniques — fake doors for demand, Wizard of Oz to fake automation with a human, concierge to deliver by hand — lets you generate evidence in days instead of betting months of engineering on a hunch.
lessons.metaphor_seed: A movie trailer is a fake-door test (release it, watch if audiences get excited before committing to the full push). A chef-run tasting menu is a concierge test (hand-made, high-touch, to learn what diners love). A carnival fortune-teller machine secretly operated by a person is a Wizard of Oz — the customer thinks it's a magic box, but it's a human, proving demand before anyone builds the machine.
lessons.case_study_seed: Dropbox's 2007-08 explainer-video test: instead of building the hard file-sync engineering first, they posted a short demo to test 'do people even feel this pain?' — the beta waitlist jumped from 5,000 to 75,000 overnight. (Alt: Zappos's Nick Swinmurn photographed shoes in local stores and only bought them after a customer ordered, testing 'will people buy shoes online?' before building any inventory system.)
lessons.pm_angle: Technical-literacy angle: most of these tests need zero production code, which is exactly where a non-technical PM can be enormously effective. But you must understand the fidelity ladder — from clickable mockup to a real working slice — and which rung answers your specific question without over-investing engineering.
deep_sections: Fake door, Wizard of Oz, concierge, smoke/landing-page tests
The prototype fidelity ladder
Matching test type to the specific risk and keeping the cost of being wrong low

modules.title: Module 3 — Vision, Strategy, and the Discipline of Saying No
modules.goal: Step up from running one team to setting direction. Learn to craft an inspiring vision, derive a focused strategy from real insights, and make the ruthless trade-offs that let a few bets win — the bridge from individual-contributor PM to product leader.

lessons.title: Product Vision: The Inspiring Future You're Creating
lessons.summary: A vision is a 5-10 year picture of how customers' lives get better, and it's the single most powerful alignment tool a leader has. Without it, every team optimizes locally and the company ships a pile of disconnected features. Vision inspires and recruits; strategy and management execute.
lessons.metaphor_seed: A product vision is the architect's finished painting of a cathedral pinned up on the construction site. No concrete is poured yet, but every stonemason, electrician, and glazier can look up, see the same spires, and decide for themselves how their piece fits — without the architect standing over each one giving orders.
lessons.case_study_seed: Amazon's 'Working Backwards': before building Kindle, AWS, and Alexa, teams wrote a fictional press release and FAQ describing the finished product from the customer's point of view, then engineered backward — documented by ex-Amazon execs Colin Bryar and Bill Carr in 'Working Backwards.'
lessons.pm_angle: Technical-literacy angle: a credible vision is anchored to what enabling technology is about to make possible. To paint a future engineers find believable rather than fantastical, you need a working sense of the tech trajectory — what's near-term feasible versus science fiction.
deep_sections: Vision vs. mission vs. strategy
Customer-improvement framing vs. company-benefit framing
Vision as a recruiting and alignment magnet

lessons.title: Insight-Driven Strategy: Choosing Which Problems to Solve
lessons.summary: Strategy is where most companies fail. Its output is not a feature list but a short, prioritized set of the most critical problems to solve to advance the vision — chosen from genuine insights mined from data, customers, enabling technology, and industry change. Insight is what separates great companies from busy ones.
lessons.metaphor_seed: Strategy is a doctor's diagnosis, not the pharmacy receipt. A weak team hands you a bag of ten medications and hopes something works; a great team runs the tests, finds the one root cause that explains your symptoms (the insight), and treats that — leaving the other nine bottles on the shelf on purpose.
lessons.case_study_seed: Netflix's data-driven strategy: viewing-completion data drove the decision to greenlight 'House of Cards,' and algorithmic per-member artwork selection was validated through hundreds of randomized A/B tests measuring lifts in click-through and play-starts — insight turned directly into strategic bets.
lessons.pm_angle: Technical-literacy angle: two of the four insight sources are quantitative data and enabling technology. Mining a real insight from data means understanding A/B testing, statistical confidence, and what your event data actually supports — and spotting which new technical capability has just unlocked a problem worth solving.
deep_sections: The four sources of insight
Strategy as the link from vision to quarterly team objectives
10x thinking vs. 10% incremental thinking

lessons.title: Ruthless Focus: The Power of Saying No
lessons.summary: Strategy is mostly about what you choose NOT to do. A roadmap stuffed with good ideas guarantees mediocrity everywhere; the highest-leverage act of leadership is deciding what to cut and defending those cuts against disappointed stakeholders. Focus is what lets a few bets get the attention needed to win.
lessons.metaphor_seed: Focus is a gardener pruning a fruit tree. Cutting off perfectly healthy, blossoming branches feels brutal — but leave them all and the tree spreads its energy thin into hundreds of tiny, sour fruit. Prune to a few branches and you get a smaller number of large, sweet apples. The pruning is the point.
lessons.case_study_seed: Steve Jobs returning to a near-bankrupt Apple in 1997: he drew a 2x2 grid (Consumer/Pro x Desktop/Portable), killed roughly 70% of the bloated product line, and committed to four products. 'Innovation is saying no to 1,000 things.' That focus underpinned Apple's recovery.
lessons.pm_angle: Technical-literacy angle: saying no credibly means understanding the engineering opportunity cost — every 'yes' consumes finite team capacity and adds long-term maintenance and complexity (technical debt). Knowing what a feature truly costs to build and keep alive lets you defend cuts with substance, not just instinct.
deep_sections: Why priority is singular by nature — you can't have five top priorities
Killing projects and sunsetting features as a leadership skill
Opportunity cost: every yes is a hidden no

lessons.title: OKRs: Measuring Impact, Not Activity
lessons.summary: OKRs (Objectives and Key Results) force the question 'did the metric we care about actually move?' An Objective is an inspirational qualitative 'what'; Key Results are the measurable outcomes that prove you got there. In the product model, leaders assign the objective (the problem) and teams propose the key results (the measures) — a dialogue, not a top-down dictation.
lessons.metaphor_seed: OKRs are a fitness goal, not a gym-attendance log. 'Go to the gym 4 times a week' is an output you can hit all year while staying unhealthy. 'Lower resting heart rate to 60 bpm and run 5km without stopping' is an outcome — it doesn't care how you train, only whether your body actually changed.
lessons.case_study_seed: John Doerr introducing OKRs to a tiny Google in 1999, carried from Intel where Andy Grove invented them and used 'Operation Crush' to out-compete Motorola. Google has used OKRs ever since to scale from a startup with no business plan — the canonical origin story from Doerr's 'Measure What Matters.'
lessons.pm_angle: Technical-literacy angle: a good key result is only as trustworthy as the instrumentation behind it. You need to know whether a metric is a leading or lagging indicator, whether it can be cleanly measured, and how to avoid disguising a task list as key results — all of which require understanding how the data is collected.
deep_sections: Objective (inspirational what) vs. key results (measurable how-we'll-know)
Cascading direction without cascading tasks
Common failure modes: too many OKRs, task lists in disguise, tying to compensation

lessons.title: Prioritization Frameworks: Defensible Trade-offs Under Constraints
lessons.summary: Even after strategy and OKRs, you face more good ideas than capacity. Frameworks like RICE turn subjective 'I feel like we should build X' arguments into structured, comparable scores that align stakeholders. The key is matching the framework to the decision type and treating the number as an input to judgment, not a verdict.
lessons.metaphor_seed: A prioritization framework is the nutrition label on packaged food. It won't decide dinner for you, but by forcing every option onto the same axes — calories, protein, sugar — it stops you being seduced by the prettiest box and lets you compare a steak and a candy bar honestly.
lessons.case_study_seed: Intercom created and popularized the RICE scoring model (Reach x Impact x Confidence / Effort) internally to decide roadmap items, then published it — it's since become one of the most widely adopted prioritization methods in product management.
lessons.pm_angle: Technical-literacy angle: the 'Effort' in RICE is an engineering estimate, and the quality of your whole prioritization depends on it being realistic. You need enough technical literacy to pressure-test effort estimates, understand why they're uncertain, and recognize when a 'small' feature hides large architectural cost.
deep_sections: RICE, ICE, Cost of Delay / WSJF, and the Kano model
Confidence as an explicit scored variable to fight overconfidence
Why frameworks inform judgment but don't replace it (garbage in, garbage out)

lessons.title: Roadmaps vs. Outcomes: Planning Honestly for Uncertainty
lessons.summary: Traditional timeline/Gantt roadmaps are treated as contracts but they're fiction — dates slip, features underperform, markets shift, and broken promises erode trust. The 2026 best practice is the outcome-based Now-Next-Later roadmap, where certainty is high for what you're building now and deliberately fuzzy further out.
lessons.metaphor_seed: An outcome roadmap is a weather forecast, not a train timetable. Tomorrow's forecast is precise and you plan around it; next week is 'probably rain'; the 10-day outlook is just 'cooler trend.' Demanding day-1 exactness for day 10 is fooling yourself — and a roadmap should be honest about fading certainty.
lessons.case_study_seed: Intercom popularized the Now-Next-Later roadmap (invented by Janna Bastow, ProdPad co-founder, in 2012), abandoning rigid quarterly Gantt commitments for three confidence-based horizons — now standard across modern product organizations.
lessons.pm_angle: Technical-literacy angle: the reason far-out dates are fiction is partly that software estimates grow exponentially less reliable with distance and discovery, especially for novel or AI work where you may train a model for weeks only to find accuracy falls short. Understanding why engineering can't promise distant dates lets you set honest expectations.
deep_sections: Now = committed solutions, Next = problems/opportunities, Later = target outcomes
Why feature/timeline roadmaps are 'false contracts' (Torres)
Managing the sales-wants-dates vs. product-needs-flexibility tension

modules.title: Module 4 — Leading People: Empowerment, Influence, and Culture
modules.goal: Learn the human side of the model — the part most transformations fail on. Build the skills to lead without authority, structure and coach empowered teams, and create the culture of innovation and execution that makes empowerment actually work.

lessons.title: The Leadership Layer: Vision, Strategy, Topology, Objectives
lessons.summary: Empowerment without direction is just chaos. Leaders create the conditions for teams to succeed by giving compelling direction (vision), making hard strategic choices, structuring teams to minimize dependencies (topology), and assigning problems via objectives — then trusting teams to find the solutions.
lessons.metaphor_seed: A great expedition leader doesn't carry every climber up Everest, nor just shout 'good luck.' They paint the vision (summit and come home alive), set the strategy (which route given the season and the team's strengths), organize who's roped to whom (topology), and assign each rope-team a clear daily objective — then trust the climbers, who can see the ice, to pick each foothold.
lessons.case_study_seed: Datasite (formerly Merrill Corporation): a 100+ year-old family-owned financial-printing business, disrupted and struggling, whose leadership set a clear vision and strategy to become a SaaS platform for the M&A community, restructured into empowered teams, and rebranded in 2020. Revenue grew 30%+ to industry leadership — a flagship leadership-driven transformation in TRANSFORMED.
lessons.pm_angle: Technical-literacy angle: 'team topology' is largely about minimizing technical dependencies between teams — you can't structure empowered teams well without understanding how the system's architecture creates or removes coupling. Conway's Law (org structure mirrors system structure) is a technical idea every product leader needs.
deep_sections: Team topology: structuring teams to minimize cross-team dependencies
Team objectives as a back-and-forth dialogue, not top-down dictation
Netflix's 'context not control' and leadership as the missing ingredient

lessons.title: Influence Without Authority: The Real PM Superpower
lessons.summary: A PM is accountable for a product's success but commands almost no one — engineers, designers, sales, legal, finance, and execs all have their own bosses and agendas. You earn influence through credibility, data, and trust rather than positional power, because a perfect strategy that can't align stakeholders ships nothing.
lessons.metaphor_seed: A product manager is the host of a dinner party, not the boss of the guests. You can't order anyone to enjoy themselves. You succeed by seating the right people together, serving what each actually likes, reading the room, and making everyone feel heard — so the whole table moves in the same happy direction without a single command.
lessons.case_study_seed: The HBR-documented near-collapse of HealthCare.gov (2013): no single accountable product owner sat across 55+ contractors and agencies, stakeholder coordination broke down, and the launch failed catastrophically — later rescued by a small 'tech surge' team that established clear ownership and decision-making.
lessons.pm_angle: Technical-literacy angle: your single most powerful currency of influence with an engineering org is technical credibility. Engineers grant influence to PMs who understand the system, respect the constraints, and can debate trade-offs — and withhold it from those who can't. Earning that credibility is a core part of building your influence.
deep_sections: Currencies of influence: expertise, data, relationships
Power-Interest (Mendelow) grid and RACI
Disagree-and-commit; building trust over time vs. winning single arguments

lessons.title: Stakeholders and the Product Model: Collaboration, Not Conquest
lessons.summary: Empowerment is not a license to ignore the business. Empowered teams still must collaborate with and respect stakeholders — legal, finance, sales, marketing — because viability risk lives in those constraints. The skill is partnering with stakeholders as a source of business knowledge rather than treating them as order-givers or obstacles.
lessons.metaphor_seed: A ship's captain has full authority over how to sail, but still must respect the harbor master, the coast guard, and maritime law. Ignoring them doesn't make the captain more empowered — it runs the ship aground. Stakeholders are the harbor conditions you navigate, not enemies to defeat.
lessons.case_study_seed: Cagan's 'Stakeholders and the Product Model' essay, contrasted with the recurring failure pattern he documents: companies that rename teams 'squads' and 'adopt OKRs' but keep handing down feature roadmaps because stakeholders never actually let go of control — vocabulary changed, behavior didn't.
lessons.pm_angle: Technical-literacy angle: viability often turns on technical-adjacent constraints stakeholders own — data privacy and security (legal), cost-of-goods and margin (finance), integration and SLAs (sales engineering). You need enough fluency to translate these constraints into real product requirements rather than discovering them after build.
deep_sections: Why viability risk lives in stakeholder constraints
Partnering with stakeholders as a knowledge source
Why transformations stall when stakeholders won't relinquish control

lessons.title: Product Culture: Innovation AND Execution
lessons.summary: A strong product culture has two halves: a culture of innovation (discovery, experimentation, appropriate risk-taking) and a culture of execution (delivery, urgency, accountability). Great companies have both, built on psychological safety, autonomy, and missionary motivation — teams that care about the mission and will push back to protect the customer.
lessons.metaphor_seed: A great product culture is like a championship sports team that both invents new plays in training (innovation) and executes flawlessly under pressure on game day (execution). A team that only experiments never wins; a team that only drills the same plays gets predictable and beaten. You need the lab and the field.
lessons.case_study_seed: Gympass (corporate wellness platform, now Wellhub), featured in TRANSFORMED: moved from a sales-led to a product-led operating model, building empowered teams and a product culture to scale globally — demonstrating both halves of culture working together.
lessons.pm_angle: Technical-literacy angle: a culture of execution depends on engineering practices that make frequent, safe releases possible — small batches, automated testing, decoupled systems, instrumentation. As a leader you can't nurture an execution culture without understanding the technical foundations (CI/CD, monitoring) that let teams ship fast without breaking things.
deep_sections: The two halves: innovation and execution
Psychological safety, autonomy, and missionary motivation as preconditions
Missionaries vs. mercenaries as a cultural state

lessons.title: Leading the Transformation: Why Most Attempts Fail
lessons.summary: Knowing the model isn't enough — most companies that try to adopt it fail, and the hardest part is people and leadership, not process. You need stronger (not fewer) leaders who coach, and you must actively manage the resistance of middle managers and stakeholders used to giving orders.
lessons.metaphor_seed: Transplanting the product model into a command-and-control company is like an organ transplant: the new organ (empowered teams) is healthy, but the host's immune system (managers and stakeholders used to control) will attack and reject it unless you actively manage the transition. Many transplants fail not because the organ was bad but because the body rejected it.
lessons.case_study_seed: The recurring cautionary pattern Cagan documents in TRANSFORMED: companies that 'adopt OKRs' or rename teams 'squads' while still handing down feature roadmaps — proof that culture and leadership, not vocabulary, decide success. Contrast with the genuine transformations (Trainline, Datasite, Almosafer) that changed how work was actually defined and assigned.
lessons.pm_angle: Technical-literacy angle: a real transformation usually requires modernizing the technical foundation too — decoupling monolithic systems so small teams can release independently. Leaders who treat transformation as purely organizational, ignoring the architecture that enforces dependencies, find the old structure reasserts itself.
deep_sections: Why you need stronger leaders, not fewer — coaching as central
The specific failure modes: renaming roles, 'doing outcomes' without transforming
Managing middle-management resistance

modules.title: Module 5 — Managing AI Products: A Different Animal
modules.goal: Build the distinct discipline of managing AI/ML products, where the engine is probabilistic, not deterministic. Learn why the old toolkit breaks, how to own measurement through evals, and how to design experiences and experiments for systems that are occasionally and unavoidably wrong.

lessons.title: From Deterministic to Probabilistic: Why AI Is Different
lessons.summary: Traditional software follows if/then rules — the same input always gives the same output. AI products are probabilistic: identical inputs can produce different outputs, and being occasionally wrong is expected behavior, not a bug. This single shift breaks the PM's usual toolkit for roadmaps, success metrics, and team composition.
lessons.metaphor_seed: Traditional software is a vending machine: press B4, you always get the same chocolate bar. An AI product is a talented but slightly unpredictable junior chef — give them the same recipe twice and you get two slightly different dishes, usually great, occasionally salty. You manage a chef by tasting the food and giving feedback, not by scripting exact muscle movements.
lessons.case_study_seed: Gartner/industry reporting through 2024-2025 that only ~48% of AI pilots reach production and at least 30% of gen-AI projects are abandoned after proof-of-concept — most often due to weak evaluation, governance, and integration rather than weak models. The 'pilot-to-production gap' that probabilistic-product discipline exists to close.
lessons.pm_angle: Technical-literacy angle: this entire module IS the technical-literacy core for AI PMs. You must understand the model lifecycle — training, monitoring, drift, retraining — as ongoing roadmap work, and that you cannot guarantee accuracy by a deadline. The team now includes data scientists and ML engineers whose work you must understand to lead.
deep_sections: Deterministic vs. probabilistic systems
Why delivery timelines for AI are non-linear and uncertain
Expanded success metrics: accuracy, fairness, explainability, trust alongside adoption

lessons.title: Evals as a PM Responsibility: Beyond the Vibe Check
lessons.summary: Because AI output is probabilistic, the only way to know whether a change made the product better or worse is a systematic evaluation — an 'eval.' This became the defining AI-PM skill of 2025-26. It's a product-judgment task, not coding: you don't write eval code, but you own deciding what to measure, curating the test set, setting thresholds, and making the ship/no-ship call.
lessons.metaphor_seed: An eval is the exam you write for your AI before letting it graduate into production. A vibe check is glancing at one homework answer and saying 'looks smart enough.' A real eval is a graded test bank of 100 questions with an answer key and a passing score — so when you swap in a new student (model or prompt), you can prove whether it actually got smarter or just got lucky.
lessons.case_study_seed: The trip-planning AI agent cited by Aman Khan (Arize) in Lenny's Newsletter that booked a user flights to San Diego instead of San Francisco — a failure a proper eval set would have caught before launch. The canonical example of why PM-owned evals beat vibe checks.
lessons.pm_angle: Technical-literacy angle: you need to understand the three eval approaches (human, code-based, LLM-as-a-judge) and their trade-offs well enough to choose. The recommended starting point — hand-label 50-100 real examples to learn what 'good' means, then automate — is a hands-on, semi-technical exercise every AI PM should be able to run.
deep_sections: Vibe checks vs. rigorous evals
The three approaches: human, code-based, LLM-as-a-judge
The four-part LLM-judge framework (Aman Khan): role, context, goal, terminology

lessons.title: Probabilistic UX: Designing for Uncertainty and Graceful Failure
lessons.summary: Classic UX assumes a predictable response to every action; AI UX must design for outputs that vary and can be wrong. Hide the uncertainty and users either over-trust a confident wrong answer or abandon the product after one bad experience. Good AI UX surfaces confidence, explains in plain language, gives override controls, and degrades gracefully.
lessons.metaphor_seed: A good GPS that re-routes you doesn't silently teleport you onto a new road — it says 'I'm 80% sure this faster route avoids traffic,' shows the alternative, and lets you decline. Probabilistic UX builds that GPS for every AI feature: it tells you how sure it is, shows its reasoning, and always leaves your hands on the wheel.
lessons.case_study_seed: GitHub Copilot's inline-suggestion design: code appears as low-commitment gray 'ghost text' the user accepts with Tab, rejects with Esc, cycles alternatives, or accepts word-by-word — a widely-studied pattern for presenting uncertain AI output as an easily-dismissable suggestion rather than a forced confident action.
lessons.pm_angle: Technical-literacy angle: surfacing 'confidence' in the UI requires understanding what confidence the model can actually report and how reliable it is, and 'agentic' experiences (where the AI takes autonomous actions) demand you grasp what the agent can do, where it can fail, and where human-in-the-loop checkpoints are technically necessary.
deep_sections: Confidence UI and plain-language explainability (microcopy)
Graceful degradation and elegant fallback states
Agentic Experience (AX): transparency and override controls for autonomous AI

lessons.title: Build-Measure-Learn with Models: Experimentation at AI Speed
lessons.summary: Coding agents and prototyping tools have collapsed the cost of building, so the bottleneck moved from 'can we build it?' to 'can we learn whether it's better?' The new advantage is learning speed: change ONE variable (prompt, model, or retrieval), measure with evals plus real-user A/B tests, and decide. Speed without discipline just lets you waste time faster.
lessons.metaphor_seed: It's the scientific method, sped up. Old development was baking a cake, waiting a week for guests to eat it, and asking if they liked it. Now you can bake a hundred mini-cakes in an afternoon — but if you change the flour, sugar, and oven temperature all at once, you'll never know which change helped. Change one ingredient at a time, and keep a reliable taste-tester.
lessons.case_study_seed: The 'Lean AI' weekly experiment cadence documented in 2025-26 practitioner playbooks, where teams use a stronger model (GPT-4-class or Claude) as an automated 'judge' to score cheaper production models — letting them iterate prompts and models in hours instead of waiting on human evaluation.
lessons.pm_angle: Technical-literacy angle: 'change one variable' means understanding the levers — prompt, model, retrieval strategy — and what each does, so you can attribute a result. Using an LLM-as-a-judge as your measurement engine, calibrated against human review, is a technical workflow you should be able to reason about and direct.
deep_sections: The Build-Measure-Learn loop adapted for probabilistic systems
LLM-as-a-judge as the fast, cheap 'Measure' engine
Coupling offline evals with online A/B tests and edge-case monitoring

lessons.title: AI-Accelerated Discovery: Faster Tests, Same Discipline
lessons.summary: The defining 2026 shift is that AI has collapsed the cost of prototyping and synthesis — tools like Figma Make, Lovable, Cursor, and Claude Code let a PM 'vibe code' a working prototype from a prompt in minutes, and AI can draft interview summaries and opportunity trees in seconds. But the human disciplines — talking to real customers, judging evidence, preserving empathy — become MORE valuable, not less.
lessons.metaphor_seed: AI is like getting a power drill after years of using a hand screwdriver. The drill makes the repetitive work astonishingly fast, but it doesn't tell you WHERE to put the holes or whether you're building the right furniture at all. The carpenter's judgment — knowing what the customer actually needs — becomes the part that matters most, precisely because everyone now has the drill.
lessons.case_study_seed: Teresa Torres building her first AI product live (covered in 2025-26 webinars and the Aakash Gupta podcast), publicly walking through how she used AI to speed synthesis and prototyping while deliberately keeping humans in the loop — and codifying a five-step discovery discipline (context engineering, orchestration, observability, evals, maintenance) specific to AI products. She notes AI summaries can miss 20-40% of important detail.
lessons.pm_angle: Technical-literacy angle: this is the exact intersection of being an AI engineer and a PM. 'Vibe coding' a testable prototype is now a PM skill, and discovery for AI products adds genuinely technical disciplines — context engineering, orchestration, observability, evals, and the error-analysis loop where humans tag failures and then write automated checks.
deep_sections: Vibe coding: building testable prototypes from plain English
What to keep human (Torres): synthesis review, judging disproof, deciding what matters
Discovery for AI products: the five-step discipline and the error-analysis loop

modules.title: Module 6 — The Business and Engineering Edge of AI Products
modules.goal: Master the hard commercial and operational realities that decide whether an AI product survives: building a real moat, pricing against non-zero compute costs, owning trust and safety as product (and legal) risk, and leading teams where AI agents write much of the code.

lessons.title: Data as the Moat: Flywheels and What Actually Compounds
lessons.summary: When any competitor can call the same foundation model and clone your feature in weeks, the feature itself is no longer defensible. What's left is what you accumulate — but a static dataset is NOT a durable moat. The real moat is a data flywheel: every user action generates signal that improves the model, which drives more usage, which generates more signal.
lessons.metaphor_seed: A data moat is like a neighborhood coffee shop that remembers every regular's order. A new chain can copy the menu and decor overnight (the features), but can't copy ten years of knowing you take oat milk on Tuesdays and your kid likes the blueberry muffin. And crucially, the shop has to actually pay attention and remember (design the flywheel), not keep a dusty guestbook nobody reads (a static dataset).
lessons.case_study_seed: Cursor (the AI code editor) as analyzed by NFX: as a whole team adopts it, the AI accumulates the team's codebase architecture, conventions, and project structure — switching to a rival means losing 'months of accumulated team knowledge embedded in the AI,' turning usage data plus workflow embedding into a compounding moat a feature-clone can't replicate.
lessons.pm_angle: Technical-literacy angle: designing a flywheel is a technical product decision — you must understand how user actions are captured as training signal, how that signal feeds model improvement, and the consent and data-governance requirements. Building the loop (vs. a dead dataset) requires real fluency in how the system learns from usage.
deep_sections: Why feature moats are dead and accumulation is the new defensibility
Static data decays vs. compounding flywheel data
NFX's six defensibilities and the motte-and-bailey strategy

lessons.title: Pricing AI Features: The New Gross-Margin Reality
lessons.summary: In classic SaaS, serving one more customer costs almost nothing, enabling per-seat pricing and 80-90% margins. AI breaks this: every query has a real compute cost, so AI businesses often run at 50-60% gross margins. PMs must rethink both how they charge (toward usage, workflow, and outcome-based models) and how they protect margin.
lessons.metaphor_seed: Old SaaS pricing was a gym membership: a flat monthly fee whether you show up or not, costing the gym nothing extra when you do. AI pricing is more like a law firm — every hour the AI 'works' costs you money, so you can bill by the hour (usage), by the case-task (workflow), or only when you win the case (outcome). Each step toward 'only when you win' is more attractive to the client but riskier for the firm.
lessons.case_study_seed: Intercom's Fin AI support agent charges $0.99 per ticket it actually resolves — per problem solved, not per message or token. The canonical outcome-based pricing example from Bessemer's playbook, alongside EvenUp (per AI-generated legal demand letter) and Leena AI (priced on tickets auto-closed).
lessons.pm_angle: Technical-literacy angle: pricing demands you model the fully-loaded cost per unit — tokens, embeddings, vector search, orchestration, monitoring — and price above a margin floor. Understanding token economics and what drives compute cost is a quantitative, technical skill, and unmonitored token costs can quietly erode margins by 6%+ if you don't track them.
deep_sections: Why AI margins are structurally lower: real per-query cost-of-goods
The pricing ladder: per-seat to usage to workflow to outcome
Cost-plus modeling and the abstraction problem (customers think in problems, not tokens)

lessons.title: Trust and Safety as a Product Concern: Hallucinations and Liability
lessons.summary: When your product can confidently state something false (a hallucination) or produce biased output, that's a product, brand, and legal risk the PM owns — courts have already held companies liable for what their AI tells customers. Trust and safety becomes a first-class requirement, designed as guardrails at three layers: input, processing, and output.
lessons.metaphor_seed: Guardrails are the bumpers in a bowling alley and the spell-checker on a press release. The AI is an eager intern who will happily send a customer a confident, totally made-up policy if you let them. You don't fire the intern — you put rules in place: check the facts before it goes out, never use that tone, never promise a refund we don't offer. Once that email leaves with your company's name on it, the company owns every word.
lessons.case_study_seed: Moffatt v. Air Canada (2024, BC Civil Resolution Tribunal): Air Canada's chatbot hallucinated a bereavement-fare refund policy contradicting the airline's own website; the tribunal rejected the 'the chatbot is a separate legal entity' defense and held the airline liable for $812.02 — the landmark case establishing companies are accountable for their AI's output.
lessons.pm_angle: Technical-literacy angle: you must understand the three guardrail layers (input filtering, processing constraints, output validation) and emerging techniques like constitutional/principle-based approaches and automated-reasoning verification well enough to specify them as requirements. Knowing where a hallucination can technically be caught lets you design defenses, not just hope.
deep_sections: Hallucinations and the three guardrail layers
Constitutional/principle-based approaches and automated-reasoning verification
Legal liability: 'you own what your chatbot says'

lessons.title: Leading Teams That Ship with Coding Agents
lessons.summary: By 2026, AI coding agents write a large share of the code, changing what the whole team optimizes for. Engineers move from writing code to orchestrating agents and verifying output — including 'hallucination checks' (did the agent invent a fake library or break the logic?). PMs get back time lost to alignment meetings but take on a sharper duty: making sure the team ships the RIGHT things, because velocity only becomes value if pointed at validated needs.
lessons.metaphor_seed: Managing an AI-native team is like running a newsroom where the junior reporters are incredibly fast AI writers. They draft articles in seconds but sometimes quote sources that don't exist. Your senior staff stop typing stories themselves and become editors — they set the style guide (AGENTS.md), fact-check every draft (the hallucination check), and decide which stories are worth running at all. You judge the newsroom not by word count but by how few errors slip into print, and whether you covered the stories readers cared about.
lessons.case_study_seed: OpenAI's 'Building an AI-Native Engineering Team' guide: Cloudwalk turns specs into working code in minutes via Codex; Sansan uses agent review to catch race conditions and database-relation bugs humans miss; Cloudflare reports its 4-week rolling average of merge requests climbing from ~5,600/week to over 8,700/week as AI tooling adoption grew.
lessons.pm_angle: Technical-literacy angle: you need to understand new artifacts like AGENTS.md (the instruction file telling agents your conventions and standards) and new metrics like 'defect-capture rate' over 'volume of commits.' Knowing that 'AGENTS.md tells agents HOW to build, but customer evidence tells the team WHAT to build' — and owning the latter — is the crux of the AI-native PM role.
deep_sections: Engineers as agent-orchestrators and verifiers; the hallucination check in code review
AGENTS.md and new metrics (defect-capture rate over commit volume)
Emerging org structures like the 'shipyard team'

lessons.title: Capstone: The AI Product Leader's Operating Loop
lessons.summary: This final lesson integrates the whole track into one continuous operating loop for an empowered AI product leader: set an insight-driven vision and strategy, run continuous discovery with the four risks, build with agents and validate with evals, design for probabilistic UX, price for real margins, guard against safety risk, and measure outcomes — then feed the learning back in. It's the full job, end to end.
lessons.metaphor_seed: Conducting an orchestra where some musicians are human virtuosos (your trio and stakeholders) and some are powerful but literal-minded AI instruments (the models and agents). The conductor doesn't play every note — they hold the score (vision and strategy), cue each section at the right moment (discovery, build, evals, launch), listen for when something's off-key (outcomes and safety), and keep the whole thing moving as one piece of music rather than a pile of solos.
lessons.case_study_seed: Synthesis across the track's flagship transformations and AI cases — Trainline and Datasite (operating model), Airbnb and Dropbox (discovery), Netflix and Apple (vision and strategy), Intercom's Fin and Cursor (AI business model), Air Canada (safety) — assembled as one decision framework an aspiring leader can apply to their own product.
lessons.pm_angle: Technical-literacy angle: the capstone ties together every technical thread — instrumentation, A/B testing, model lifecycle, evals, token economics, guardrails, and agent orchestration — into the integrated judgment that lets a non-technical-by-background leader operate credibly as both a great PM and a great AI engineer's true partner.
deep_sections: The end-to-end operating loop, stage by stage
Where the technical and product disciplines reinforce each other
Cagan's 2025-26 provocation: as AI absorbs the narrow 'Product Owner' role, real product judgment becomes the differentiator

gaps: NO ACTUAL CODING ANYWHERE. The Engineering track promises the learner can 'design, ship' AI features and 'hold your own with senior AI engineers,' but no lesson teaches programming, calling an LLM API, reading/writing JSON, HTTP requests, Python, environment setup, or even pseudocode. A smart non-technical person finishing both tracks would be deeply conceptually fluent yet unable to build a single working thing. For the 'great AI engineer' half of the promise this is the load-bearing gap. (The home CLAUDE.md shows a separate 'Python 101' course exists - the curriculum must explicitly sequence it as a prerequisite or Module 0, not silently assume it.)

No 'AI fundamentals before LLMs' grounding. The Engineering track opens at next-token prediction with zero coverage of what machine learning even is (data -> model -> prediction), supervised vs unsupervised learning, what 'training on data' means mechanically, or neural networks at a metaphor level. For a non-technical learner, jumping straight to 'parameters' and 'embeddings' skips the conceptual floor those ideas stand on.

Multimodality is barely present. In 2026 vision, audio, image-generation, speech, and video models are central to both engineering and product work. Embeddings lesson mentions images/audio in one bullet; otherwise the entire curriculum is text-LLM-centric. No lesson on multimodal inputs/outputs, OCR/document AI, voice agents, or image generation as a product surface.

Data engineering and the data layer are underbuilt for an 'AI engineer.' RAG is covered well, but there's almost nothing on data pipelines, data quality/labeling, ETL, where training/eval data comes from, synthetic data, or data versioning - the unglamorous 80% of real ML work.

Ethics, bias, fairness, and responsible AI get only glancing mention (one bullet in Product M5 success metrics; 'ethical' as one of five assumption categories). For someone becoming a product LEADER shipping AI to real people, there is no dedicated treatment of bias/fairness, disparate impact, dataset representativeness, or the reputational/discrimination risks that have produced major real-world failures.

Regulation and compliance are nearly absent. No EU AI Act, no risk tiers, no GDPR/data-residency depth, no copyright/IP-of-training-data issues, no sector rules (HIPAA/finance). This is a top-3 concern for any SV product leader shipping AI in 2026 and a real engineering constraint (what data you can use, what you must log).

No coverage of the model/vendor landscape and procurement as a strategic skill. Open vs closed weights is touched in M6, but there is no clear map of the provider ecosystem (OpenAI/Anthropic/Google/Meta/open-source), how to choose/switch providers, avoid lock-in, or run the build-vs-buy decision - a core PM-and-engineer judgment call.

Product track has no quantitative/analytics foundation despite repeatedly insisting on 'deep knowledge of the data.' Every PM lesson says metrics matter and instrumentation matters, but no lesson teaches the actual literacy: reading an A/B test, statistical significance, funnels/cohorts/retention curves, leading vs lagging indicators, or SQL/dashboard basics. The track tells the learner this skill is essential but never teaches it.

Go-to-market, growth, and the commercial side are thin in the Product track. There is strong discovery/strategy coverage but little on growth loops, acquisition/activation/retention (the AARRR funnel), positioning, sales-product collaboration beyond 'stakeholders,' or how AI products actually reach users. A 'great SV product leader' needs the growth toolkit.

Neither track teaches version control / Git, the development workflow, CI/CD beyond a passing mention, or how software actually gets shipped end to end. For someone meant to credibly partner with (or be) an engineer and lead an 'agentic SDLC,' the basic mechanics of the software lifecycle are assumed, not taught.

Latency/performance engineering and scaling/infrastructure (GPUs, serving, throughput, rate limits, caching beyond semantic cache) are mentioned as concerns but never explained at a foundational level - a gap for the 'engineer' identity.
accessibility_notes: Engineering Module 1 front-loads a dense pileup of technical primitives - tokens, context windows, embeddings, vectors/cosine similarity, parameters, temperature/sampling, pretraining/SFT/RLHF - in the first ~5 lessons. The metaphors are excellent, but a non-technical learner will hit 'cosine similarity,' 'quadratic attention,' and 'parameter-efficient methods (LoRA)' with no math floor beneath them. Risk of early overwhelm right where confidence should be built.

Acronym density is high and many are introduced fast: RAG, RLHF, SFT, RLAIF, MCP, ACI, CRAG, SDLC/A-SDLC, COGS, WSJF, RICE, OKR, RAT, AARRR, pass^k. Each may be defined once, but the cumulative load on a non-technical reader is heavy. The curriculum needs a running glossary and a 'no unexplained jargon' enforcement pass.

Several 'deep_sections' silently assume programming/CS background the learner does not have: 'constrained decoding,' 'JSON Schema strict mode,' 'function calling: how the model asks your code to run something,' 'traces and spans,' 'OpenTelemetry conventions.' Without prior exposure to what a function, a schema, or an API call IS, these will read as opaque. They presuppose the missing coding/AI-fundamentals modules.

The Engineering track assumes the learner knows what JSON, an API, a database, a pull request, a Linux shell, and a 'stack trace' are - these appear in metaphors and case studies (SWE-agent's Linux shell, 'no stack trace,' 'returns a pull request') as if familiar. A non-technical learner will quietly lose the thread.

Product track is far more accessible than Engineering (better-grounded metaphors, less jargon) - the difficulty curve between the two tracks is steep and uneven. A learner doing Engineering M1-M3 then Product M1 will feel whiplash in required background.

Some case studies assume domain context a non-technical learner lacks: 'Nvidia's stock dropped,' 'SWE-bench Verified ~80.8%,' 'pass^k metric,' 'Conway's Law.' These are name-dropped as self-evidently meaningful. Each needs a one-line 'why this number matters' unpack to stay in plain English.
sequencing_notes: Add a Module 0 to the Engineering track (or make Python 101 an explicit gate): basic coding literacy + 'what is machine learning' + what an API/JSON/HTTP is. Everything from Module 1 onward implicitly depends on these. As written, the very first technical lesson assumes them.

The two tracks have no defined interleaving or ordering relative to each other, yet they cross-reference the same concepts (data flywheel appears as Eng M7 L1 AND Product M6 L1; RAG/grounding/Air Canada appears in Eng M2, Eng M6, and Product M6; hallucination appears in Eng M1, Eng M2, and Product M6). The learner needs an explicit 'do these in this braided order' map so reinforcement lands instead of feeling like repetition.

Within Engineering M1, consider moving 'Capabilities, Limits, and the Map' (currently last) earlier, or splitting it: it's the most intuitive, motivating framing and would make a gentler on-ramp than opening on next-token probability distributions. The strongest motivating 'why care' lesson sits at the end of the module.

Evals (Eng M4) are introduced AFTER agents (Eng M3), but evals are the prerequisite discipline for trusting anything you build - including the simpler features in M2. Consider seeding a lightweight 'how do you even know it works?' concept before/within M2 so the learner isn't building for two modules with no notion of measurement.

In the Product track, 'Managing AI Products' (M5) and 'Business/Engineering Edge of AI' (M6) come last, after a learner has done the entire generic-PM arc. That's defensible, but the AI-specific evals lesson (Product M5) heavily duplicates Engineering M4 - the cross-track sequencing should have the learner do Engineering's eval module FIRST and treat Product M5's eval lesson as the 'PM-ownership' framing, not a re-teach.

Safety/governance (Eng M5) currently sits before Production/LLMOps (Eng M6), but several governance controls (sandboxing, least privilege, human-in-the-loop) are operational/production concerns. The split is reasonable, but flag the dependency: M5's 'excessive agency' and blast-radius ideas lean on tool-use (M2) and agents (M3), which is fine, but the governance-of-agents lesson should explicitly come after the autonomy-dial lesson it references.
redundancy: Data flywheel / moat is taught twice as a near-duplicate capstone-level lesson: Engineering M7 L1 (snowball, Tesla) and Product M6 L1 (coffee shop, Cursor/NFX). Same concept, same 'three ingredients,' overlapping sources. Consolidate into one canonical lesson owned by the Product track, and have Engineering reference it.

RAG is covered three times with heavy overlap: Engineering M2 L2 ('open-book exam', RAG poisoning), Engineering M6 L4 ('open-book exam at scale', Air Canada), and the grounding angle reappears in Product M6 L3 (Air Canada again). The 'open-book exam' metaphor and the Air Canada case are both reused. The M2-vs-M6 split (intro vs production) is justifiable, but the metaphor and case-study reuse should be deliberate callbacks, not apparent copy-paste.

Hallucination + the certainty/UX response appears in Engineering M1 L5 (Mata v. Avianca), is previewed again across M2/M4/M5, and is re-taught in Product M6 L3 (Air Canada, 'eager intern'). The 'eager intern' metaphor is itself reused for BOTH hallucination (Product M6 L3) and prompt injection (Eng M5 L1) - two different concepts sharing one metaphor risks blurring them.

LLM-as-a-judge is taught in Engineering M4 L4 (full bias taxonomy) and again in Product M5 L2 + L4 (four-part judge framework). Acceptable as engineer-depth vs PM-ownership, but the overlap should be explicitly cross-referenced so it reads as one concept seen twice, not two introductions.

Evals broadly: Engineering M4 (whole module) and Product M5 L2 cover the same core idea (evals are the new unit tests / beyond vibe checks) with different sources. Strong reinforcement if sequenced; redundant if not flagged.

Air Canada and Mata v. Avianca are each used as the marquee case more than once. Powerful cases, but reusing the same two failures across multiple lessons can make the curriculum feel narrower in its evidence base than it is - vary the examples or make reuse an intentional 'remember this case' callback.

Token economics / per-use cost appears in Eng M1 L2, Eng M6 L3 ('toll bridge vs taxi meter'), and Product M6 L2 ('gym vs law firm'). Three good metaphors for one idea - reinforcement is fine but should be acknowledged as the same lesson deepening, not three separate discoveries.
suggested_additions: A hands-on 'build a tiny AI feature' thread running through the Engineering track: by end of M2 the learner should have called a real model API, gotten JSON back, and wired up a trivial RAG over a few documents - even via no-code/low-code or guided 'vibe coding.' Concepts without a single build artifact won't produce a 'great AI engineer.'

A dedicated Responsible AI / Ethics / Fairness lesson (and a Regulation & Compliance lesson: EU AI Act risk tiers, GDPR/data residency, copyright of training data, sector rules). These belong in BOTH tracks - engineering for what it constrains technically, product for the risk/liability ownership.

A 'Quantitative & Analytics Literacy for PMs' lesson early in the Product track: A/B testing and significance, funnels, cohorts, retention, leading vs lagging metrics, and basic SQL/dashboard reading - so the repeated 'deep knowledge of the data' claim is actually taught, not just asserted.
A multimodal lesson: vision/document AI, speech/voice agents, and image/video generation as product surfaces and engineering inputs - given how central these are by 2026.

A growth & go-to-market lesson in the Product track: acquisition/activation/retention loops, positioning, pricing-meets-distribution, and product-led growth - the commercial muscle a great SV leader needs beyond discovery and strategy.
A 'how software actually ships' primer: version control/Git, environments, CI/CD, what a deploy/rollback is - prerequisite literacy for credibly leading an agentic SDLC and partnering with engineers.
A model/provider landscape + build-vs-buy + lock-in lesson: who the players are, open vs closed tradeoffs, switching costs, and how to make the procurement call - a shared engineer/PM judgment skill.

A glossary + spaced-recall layer across both tracks (running glossary, end-of-lesson plain-English recap of every new term) to enforce the 'no unexplained jargon' promise for a non-technical audience.

An explicit cross-track 'braided' learning path that interleaves the two tracks so shared concepts (evals, RAG, flywheel, hallucination, token economics) are taught once at depth and then deliberately reinforced from the other discipline's angle.
critique.overall_verdict: STRONG conceptual curriculum, INCOMPLETE for the stated dual goal. This is genuinely impressive work: modern (consistently 2025-26 dated), well-sourced, and built on an excellent repeatable pedagogy (plain-English summary + concrete metaphor + real case study + PM angle + deep sections). The Engineering track is one of the best CONCEPTUAL maps of modern applied LLM/agent work I've seen, and the Product track is a faithful, well-grounded Cagan/Torres-based PM education with a smart AI-products extension. Cross-track themes (evals, flywheel, RAG, economics, trust) are chosen well and would reinforce each other if sequenced deliberately. BUT it does not yet deliver on half its own promise. The phrase 'great AI ENGINEER' is not earned by any lesson: there is zero programming, zero API/JSON/HTTP mechanics, no ML fundamentals floor, and no single thing the learner actually builds - it produces a fluent, credible NON-builder who can hold a conversation but not ship code. For a smart non-technical learner specifically, Module 1 of the Engineering track also front-loads too much jargon (cosine similarity, quadratic attention, LoRA, constrained decoding) without the prerequisite coding/ML floor, risking early overwhelm. And several genuinely essential 2026 topics are missing or glancing: responsible AI/bias/fairness, regulation/compliance (EU AI Act, GDPR, copyright), multimodality, data engineering, PM quantitative/analytics literacy, and growth/GTM. RECOMMENDATION: (1) add a coding + ML-fundamentals Module 0 (or hard-gate the existing Python 101) plus a hands-on 'build a tiny AI feature' thread, (2) add the missing ethics/regulation, multimodal, analytics-literacy, and growth lessons, (3) consolidate the duplicated flywheel/RAG/eval/hallucination content into single canonical lessons with deliberate cross-references, and (4) define an explicit braided ordering across the two tracks with a running glossary. With those fixes it becomes genuinely comprehensive for producing both a great AI engineer AND a great SV product leader; as written, it reliably produces the second and the conceptual-but-non-building shell of the first.
